photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: yellow, black, and teal commercial facade with a silver graffiti-covered shutter and large black roof signs
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Storefront is active but weathered, with layered signs, graffiti on the shutter, and worn trim.
photo_observations.window_details: No conventional front windows are visible; the frontage is mostly a roll-down shutter and sign bands.
photo_observations.door_details: A side entry is present beside the shutter but is partly obscured by parked vehicles and the neighboring stall.
photo_observations.cornice_details: Flat canopy and oversized signboards create the visual cornice line.
photo_observations.decorative_details: Large rooftop dealer signs and a painted shutter dominate the facade.
photo_observations.storefront_description: Workwear storefront with a shuttered opening, big overhead signs, and little visible glazing.
photo_observations.signage_observed: HARRY DAVID LTD, PHOENIX, 220 Augusta Ave, Monday to Saturday 9AM to 6PM.
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Storefront heavily altered with roll-down shutter, oversized signs, and graffiti.
photo_observations.notes: Best frontal view of the signed storefront.

facade_description: 220 Augusta Ave presents a converted house-form, semi-detached facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its converted house-form, semi-detached typology. The facade reads primarily as mixed in a yellow, black, and teal commercial facade with a silver graffiti-covered shutter and large black roof signs palette, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 0 window bays from lower to upper levels, using storefront, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that has been modified to accommodate a commercial conversion through ground floor alterations. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s
